name: Human Thistlethwaite (HTA)
description: A human-adapted version of Morwen Thistlethwaite's famous computer algorithm. The original algorithm was the first to prove the cube could always be solved in a bounded number of moves by progressively restricting the available move set. The Human Thistlethwaite Algorithm (HTA) translates this concept into four manageable phases that a human can execute, reducing the cube step by step until it can be solved with only half-turn moves.

steps.name: Phase 1: Edge Orientation (EO)
steps.description: Orient all 12 edges so that they can be solved without using quarter-turn F or B moves. This restricts the cube from the full move group <R, L, U, D, F, B> to <R, L, U, D, F2, B2>.
tips: Learn to identify 'bad' edges that need flipping
Use F and B moves to flip edges, then avoid them afterwards
Similar to EO step in ZZ method

steps.name: Phase 2: Corner Orientation + E-slice Separation
steps.description: Orient all 8 corners and move the 4 E-slice (equatorial) edges into the E-slice (they do not need to be permuted correctly yet). After this phase the cube is restricted to <R, L, U2, D2, F2, B2>.
tips: Corner orientation uses R and L moves with U2/D2 adjustments
Focus on getting E-slice edges into the middle layer
This is the most complex phase to learn

steps.name: Phase 3: Orbit Placement
steps.description: Place all pieces into their correct orbits: corners into their correct tetrads, edges into their correct slices, and fix corner parity. After this phase, the cube is restricted to <R2, L2, U2, D2, F2, B2> (half turns only).
tips: Think of corners in two groups of four (tetrads)
Edges must be in their home slice
This phase establishes the domino reduction state

steps.name: Phase 4: Half-Turn Solve
steps.description: Solve the cube completely using only half-turn (180-degree) moves. Since all pieces are in their correct orbits, the cube can be solved with only R2, L2, U2, D2, F2, B2 moves.
tips: Limited number of cases in the half-turn group
Algorithms are easy to execute quickly
Can be solved with pattern recognition and short sequences

pros: Methodical and logical approach to solving
Each phase strictly reduces the problem
cons: Steep learning curve for understanding the theory
Few dedicated tutorials compared to mainstream methods
Phase 2 and 3 recognition can be challenging
creator: Morwen Thistlethwaite (adapted for human use)
